posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Decanted for two hours, side by side with a 1989 Chateau Meyney that is firing on all cyclinders, and this Forman has me fooled for Bordeaux. Started with a notable, Togni-like presence of bell pepper, but has morphed into an earth-driven, dark-fruited wine with a musky perfume and truffle. Such impeccable balance with the ripeness of fruit. Really excellent. (93 pts.)

The 89 Meyney is beyond amazement. Such basic pedigree so out-kicking its coverage. Typical Cordier of the 1980s. Bordeaux funk, sous bois, dank undergrowth and ripe tobacco. Another dark fruited-beauty. Killer acid. (94 pts.)

Two gorgeous wines. Paired with grilled rack of lamb.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Ric has really excelled this decade, and the 2011 won’t disappoint. I actually marginally prefer it to his '12. The '10, '13 and '14 are also all worthy of pursuit. I haven’t been such a fan of his bottlings from the mid-2000s, when it seems to me as if he was pushing the envelope more in the cellar and playing around with more elevated levels of ripeness, before finding (or perhaps returning to) a nice equilibrium from 2009 or so on. To me, vintages such 2008, 2007, 2005 and 2004 don’t have the harmony or the savory nuance of what came later, or what came before; 06 and 03 being partial exceptions. Of course, the vintages 1990s were very strong and have generally aged well.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
Marc Frontario:

To me 2014 Meyney is not like the 80’s versions, yet not overly new world either.
My sample was tight, but expressed alot of structure and ageablity. I would like to try this again with some years under its belt

how old were you in 1992?

I agree with you. I think it is a bit different (and I did drink 85-90 Meyney at three years of age). It’s a bit more polished and shows a little oak, but I found it very attractive and not out of place alongside a 1988. I’m not sure if the 2015 or 2016 might be heavy or too ripe, but I think 2014 was a good year for this style.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: We have the highest taxes in the US on spirits, wine isn’t much better, but the Kroger chain QFC has some unreal wines if you shop them…have found 2012 Forman, Tremblay, 1996 Krug and Salon, Sandrone and Giacosa…just depends on what the steward liked. The new stewards are bonuses on inventory, so happy to let loose at original cost or a loss.
